title: Incident Response Tabletop
description: Run a tabletop exercise for a building incident, define the command structure, and produce a communication and closeout plan.
whyItMatters: Crisis performance is mostly built before the crisis arrives. Tabletop exercises expose gaps in contact lists, decision authority, contractor readiness, and communication habits while the cost of learning is still low.

steps.title: Choose the incident scenario
steps.description: Select a realistic event such as a burst pipe, extended power outage, major lift failure, security breach, or fire panel activation with tenant disruption.
steps.tips: Pick a scenario that would genuinely stretch the property team, not just a routine work order.

steps.title: Map the RACI
steps.description: Using the Incident Command RACI, assign who leads, who approves emergency spend, who updates tenants, who handles contractors, and who records the timeline.
steps.tips: Tabletops become useful when ownership is specific down to named functions.

steps.title: Write the communication sequence
steps.description: Draft the first tenant notice, owner escalation, internal update cadence, and criteria for when communications widen to legal, security, or executive stakeholders.
steps.tips: Speed matters, but so does confidence. Aim for clear and credible messages.

steps.title: Complete the closeout review
steps.description: Document lessons learned, missing controls, contractor issues, and process changes that should be made after the exercise.
steps.tips: The closeout is where preparedness becomes lasting operational improvement.

deliverables: A closeout review with control improvements

rubric.description: The response plan makes ownership, escalation, and decision rights unmistakable.
Messages are timely, practical, and suited to stressed stakeholders.
The scenario and actions reflect how a real property team would need to respond.
The closeout identifies concrete process or control improvements rather than vague lessons.
